section.title: Doing Good to All
section.sectionRationale: The life of the Spirit works itself out in community. The spiritual restore the fallen gently, carry one another's burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ, while each also carries his own load before God. Paul warns that God is not mocked: we reap what we sow, whether to the flesh or to the Spirit. So believers must not grow weary in well-doing but do good to everyone, especially the household of faith.
section.thesis: Those who walk by the Spirit bear one another's burdens and so fulfill the law of Christ, testing their own work rather than comparing, for God is not mocked and each reaps what he sows; therefore believers must sow to the Spirit and never tire of doing good to all, especially to the family of faith.
section.frameNote: Three movements: restoring the fallen and bearing one another's burdens to fulfill the law of Christ; the sober principle that each reaps what he sows, to the flesh or to the Spirit; and the call not to grow weary but to do good to everyone, especially the household of faith.

units.label: Bear one another's burdens
units.note: restore the fallen and fulfill the law of Christ

verses.text: Brothers, if someone is caught in a trespass, you who are spiritual should restore him with a spirit of {{prautes|gentleness}}. But watch yourself, or you also may be tempted.
Carry one another's {{baros|burdens}}, and in this way you will fulfill the law of Christ.
If anyone thinks he is something when he is nothing, he deceives himself.
Each one should test his own work. Then he will have reason to boast in himself alone, and not in someone else.
For each one should carry his own load.
Nevertheless, the one who receives instruction in the word must share in all good things with his instructor.

units.label: You reap what you sow
units.note: to the flesh or to the Spirit

verses.text: Do not be deceived: God is not to be mocked. Whatever a man {{speiro|sows}}, he will reap in return.
The one who sows to please his flesh, from the flesh will reap destruction; but the one who sows to please the Spirit, from the Spirit will reap eternal life.
Let us not grow weary in well-doing, for in due time we will reap a harvest if we do not give up.
Therefore, as we have opportunity, let us do good to everyone, and especially to the family of faith.

prautes.gloss: Gentleness, meekness, strength under control. It is one of the fruits of the Spirit, and here it is the spirit in which the spiritual are to restore a fallen brother, not with harshness or superiority but with tender, humble care, watchful of their own weakness. Gentleness marks the community shaped by the Spirit rather than by rivalry.

baros.gloss: A weight, a heavy burden too great to carry alone. Believers are to carry one another's baros, sharing the crushing loads of sin, sorrow, and struggle, and in doing so they fulfill the law of Christ, the law of love. This is distinct from the load each must carry in verse 5; the heavy burden is shared, the personal responsibility is owned.

speiro.gloss: To sow, to scatter seed. Paul draws on the farming truth that the harvest matches the seed: sow to the flesh and reap destruction, sow to the Spirit and reap eternal life. The word makes daily choices weighty, for each is a seed with a certain harvest, and God is not mocked, so the outcome is sure.

basic.context: Life in the Spirit is not private; it shapes how believers treat each other. Paul tells the spiritual to restore those who fall, gently and humbly, and to carry one another's heavy burdens, which fulfills the law of Christ. Yet each is also responsible before God for his own life. Then he gives a farming law of the soul: you reap what you sow. So keep sowing to the Spirit and never tire of doing good to all.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the Spirit filled life worked out in community. Read the seeming tension between bear one another's burdens and each carry his own load as two truths held together: shared support and personal responsibility. The sowing and reaping principle grounds everyday choices in eternal outcomes.
basic.originalLanguages: The fallen are to be restored with prautes, gentleness, a fruit of the Spirit. Believers carry one another's baros, heavy burdens, fulfilling the law of Christ. And the law of the harvest is stated with speiro, to sow: what you sow, to flesh or Spirit, you will reap.
basic.typology: The law of Christ, fulfilled in bearing burdens, is the law of love that Christ Himself embodied by bearing our burdens at the cross. Sowing and reaping echoes the wisdom of Scripture and points to the final harvest, the day of judgment and reward that runs through the whole Bible.
basic.christ: The law of Christ is the pattern of Jesus who bore our burdens and laid down His life in love. To carry one another's burdens is to walk in His way. The eternal life reaped by sowing to the Spirit is the life Christ secured and gives, the harvest of His finished work applied by the Spirit.
basic.god: God is not mocked: He sees and He is just, and the harvest of every life matches its sowing. He is the God who rewards persevering good and who gives eternal life to those who sow to the Spirit. His moral order is sure, so no one deceives Him and no good done in faith is wasted.
basic.crossReferences: John 13 gives the new command to love as Christ loved, the law of Christ. 2 Corinthians 9 develops sowing and reaping in generosity. James 5 speaks of restoring the wanderer.
basic.oneStory: The gospel creates a new community that lives by the Spirit and loves like Christ. Bearing burdens and doing good are the shape of the new creation life breaking into the present. The sure harvest points forward to the final day when God gathers the fruit of grace and gives eternal life.

context.literary: This section applies the life of the Spirit from chapter 5 to concrete community relationships. It moves from restoring the fallen, to the balance of shared burdens and personal responsibility, to the universal law of sowing and reaping, and closes with the summons to do good to all. The apparent paradox of verses 2 and 5 is resolved by the different words for burden.
context.historical: The Galatian churches were being torn by the controversy, with rivalry, conceit, and division among them. Paul's call to gentle restoration and burden bearing addresses a community under strain. The instruction to share with teachers of the word shows the practical support of gospel ministry in the early church.
context.canonical: The law of Christ names the ethic of love that fulfills the law, tying together Jesus' new command and Paul's teaching. The principle of sowing and reaping runs through Scripture's wisdom and its vision of final judgment, grounding Christian perseverance in the certainty of God's harvest.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show how the Spirit filled life is lived out in community through gentle restoration, burden bearing, and doing good, and to ground perseverance in good works in the sure principle that one reaps what one sows, whether to the flesh or to the Spirit.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is largely prescriptive: restore, carry burdens, test your own work, do good. The sowing and reaping principle describes God's sure moral order and functions as a motive and warning for the commands.

debates.question: How do bear one another's burdens and carry your own load fit together?

views.label: Different burdens, shared and personal
views.case: The heavy, crushing burden of verse 2 is to be shared, while the load of verse 5 is each person's own responsibility before God that cannot be transferred; the two commands are complementary, not contradictory.

views.label: A caution against dependence
views.case: Some stress verse 5 as a guard against laziness, but the point is personal accountability alongside genuine mutual support, not a limit on compassion.

debates.bearing: The Spirit filled community both carries each other's crushing loads and owns individual responsibility before God; love and accountability belong together.

debates.question: Does sowing and reaping teach salvation by works?

views.label: The fruit and evidence of grace
views.case: Sowing to the Spirit is the life produced by grace through faith; the harvest of eternal life is God's gift, and the good works are the sure evidence of the Spirit's presence, not the earning of salvation.

views.label: A works based warning
views.case: Read alone the verse might sound like earning, but in the letter's argument it describes the pattern of a life shaped by the Spirit, whose end is eternal life.

debates.bearing: The passage does not overturn justification by faith; it shows that genuine faith sows to the Spirit and reaps the harvest God graciously gives.

study.originalLanguages: The community shaped by the Spirit is marked by three things Paul names. First, prautes, gentleness, the fruit of the Spirit in which the strong restore the fallen, humble and watchful of their own weakness. Second, baros, the heavy burden that no one should carry alone, which believers share and so fulfill the law of Christ, the law of love. Third, speiro, to sow, the farming picture that makes every choice a seed: sow to the flesh and reap destruction, sow to the Spirit and reap eternal life. God is not mocked, so the harvest is certain, which turns daily faithfulness into something weighty and hopeful.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the pattern of Jesus who bore our burdens and laid down His life in love, now reproduced in His people who carry one another's burdens.
Echoes the wisdom of Scripture and points to the final harvest, the day of judgment and reward, when God gathers the fruit of every life.

study.christ: The law of Christ that believers fulfill by bearing burdens is the pattern of Jesus Himself, who bore our sins and sorrows and laid down His life in love. To carry one another's burdens is to walk in His footsteps and reproduce His self giving. And the eternal life reaped by those who sow to the Spirit is the very life Christ secured by His death and resurrection, given by the Spirit and gathered at the final harvest. The whole ethic flows from and returns to Christ.
study.god: God is not mocked, verse 7, a solemn declaration that He sees every life clearly and judges justly, so that no one can deceive Him with mere appearances. He is the God who ensures that each reaps what he sows, and who gives eternal life to those who sow to the Spirit. He rewards persevering good done in faith and lets no such labor go to waste. His sure moral order undergirds the call to keep doing good.

crossReferences.note: The new command to love one another as Christ loved, the law of Christ fulfilled in burden bearing.
The one who sows generously will reap generously, developing the sowing principle in giving.
Turning back a wandering brother, akin to gently restoring the fallen.
Christ who carries our burdens and gives rest, the model of the law of Christ.

study.oneStory: The gospel does not save isolated individuals but forms a new family that lives by the Spirit and loves as Christ loved. Bearing burdens, restoring the fallen, and doing good to all are the life of the new creation breaking into the present. And the sure law of the harvest points ahead to the last day, when God gathers the fruit of His grace and gives eternal life to all who, by the Spirit, have sown to Him and not given up.

sermon.title: Sowing and Reaping (Galatians 6:1 to 10)
sermon.why: Keller draws out how the Spirit filled life shapes the community, in gentle restoration and burden bearing, and how the principle of sowing and reaping calls believers to keep sowing to the Spirit and doing good to all.

quiz.q: How should the spiritual restore someone caught in sin?

options.text: Gently, watching themselves lest they also be tempted.
options.note: Verse 1 calls for a spirit of gentleness and self awareness.

options.text: Harshly, to make an example of them.
options.note: Restoration is to be gentle, not harsh.

options.text: By ignoring the sin to keep the peace.
options.note: The goal is to restore, not to ignore the trespass.

options.text: By expelling them permanently.
options.note: The aim is gentle restoration, not permanent expulsion.

quiz.q: What does it mean to fulfill the law of Christ?

options.text: To carry one another's burdens in love.
options.note: Verse 2 links burden bearing to the law of Christ.

options.text: To keep the law of Moses perfectly.
options.note: The law of Christ is the law of love, not the Mosaic system.

options.text: To avoid all contact with the fallen.
options.note: The opposite; it means gently restoring and helping them.

options.text: To boast in one's own achievements.
options.note: Verse 4 warns against boastful comparison, not this.

quiz.q: What is the principle of sowing and reaping?

options.text: Whatever a person sows, to flesh or Spirit, he will reap in return.
options.note: Verses 7 and 8 state the sure law of the harvest.

options.text: That our choices have no real consequences.
options.note: The opposite; God is not mocked and the harvest is certain.

options.text: That only good people ever face any harvest.
options.note: Both kinds of sowing yield a harvest, destruction or life.

options.text: That God can be fooled by appearances.
options.note: God is not mocked; He sees and judges truly.

quiz.q: What does one reap who sows to please the Spirit?

options.text: Eternal life from the Spirit.
options.note: Verse 8 promises the harvest of eternal life.

options.text: Nothing of lasting value.
options.note: The harvest is eternal life, of the greatest value.

options.text: Only earthly comfort.
options.note: The promised reaping is eternal life, not merely comfort.

options.text: Destruction, the same as the flesh.
options.note: Destruction is the harvest of the flesh, not the Spirit.

quiz.q: To whom should believers do good?

options.text: To everyone, and especially to the family of faith.
options.note: Verse 10 sets the scope and the priority.

options.text: Only to fellow believers.
options.note: The good is to everyone, with special care for the household of faith.

options.text: Only to those who can repay them.
options.note: The call is to do good to all, not only to those who repay.

options.text: To no one, since good works do not matter.
options.note: Paul urges not growing weary in well-doing.

questions: Paul says to restore the fallen gently, watching yourself. How does remembering your own weakness change the way you approach someone else's failure?
Believers are to carry one another's heavy burdens. Whose burden could you help carry this week, and whose help might you need to receive?
God is not mocked; we reap what we sow. What are you currently sowing in your daily choices, and what harvest is it likely to bring?
Paul urges us not to grow weary in doing good. Where are you tired of doing good, and what would help you keep sowing to the Spirit?
We are to do good especially to the family of faith. How is your church family actually caring for its own, and what part could you play?

translationNotes.note: Burdens renders baros, a heavy, crushing weight to be shared; it is distinct from the load of verse 5, so the two verses are complementary rather than contradictory.
The law of Christ is the law of love that Christ taught and embodied, fulfilled as believers bear one another's burdens.
God is not to be mocked translates a phrase meaning God will not be treated with contempt or outwitted; the harvest matches the sowing with certainty.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 6:1-10. No em dashes in this passage required substitution. Sermon verified this session from the Gospel in Life series Galatians: New Freedom, New Family by Tim Keller.
